case_definitions.name: browser-headless
case_definitions.task: Open http://localhost:3000, click through the signup flow, and capture screenshots of any bug evidence.

case_definitions.name: qa-fix
case_definitions.task: QA this staging site, fix the bugs you find, add regression tests, and commit each fix atomically.

case_definitions.name: qa-report-only
case_definitions.task: Test this app and give me a bug report with repro steps, but do not edit any files.

case_definitions.name: implemented-design-polish
case_definitions.task: Audit the implemented dashboard UI for spacing, hierarchy, and visual slop, then fix the issues.

case_definitions.name: design-plan-review
case_definitions.task: Review this UI plan before implementation and score the design dimensions with recommendations.

case_definitions.name: design-system
case_definitions.task: Create a design system and DESIGN.md for a new B2B workflow product.

case_definitions.name: design-variants
case_definitions.task: Show me several visual directions for this feature and let me compare options before choosing.

case_definitions.name: product-brainstorm
case_definitions.task: I have an idea for a restaurant waitlist tool. Help me pressure-test whether it is worth building.

case_definitions.name: spec-plus-browser-validation
case_definitions.task: Turn this rough feature request into a spec, pressure-test it, then validate the critical path in the browser.

case_definitions.name: ceo-scope-review
case_definitions.task: Rethink this plan from a founder perspective and tell me whether the scope is ambitious enough.

case_definitions.name: engineering-plan-review
case_definitions.task: Review the architecture, data flow, edge cases, and test plan before implementation.

case_definitions.name: auto-plan-review
case_definitions.task: Run CEO, design, and engineering plan reviews automatically and surface only the key decisions.

case_definitions.name: pre-landing-review
case_definitions.task: Review this PR before merge for SQL safety, trust boundaries, and production bugs.

case_definitions.name: root-cause-debug
case_definitions.task: Debug why this job started failing and do not patch anything until you find the root cause.

case_definitions.name: security-audit
case_definitions.task: Run an infrastructure-first security audit with secrets, dependency supply chain, OWASP, and STRIDE coverage.

case_definitions.name: ship-pr
case_definitions.task: Sync main, run tests, update changelog, push this branch, and create a PR.

case_definitions.name: land-and-deploy
case_definitions.task: Merge the approved PR, wait for CI and deploy, then verify production health.

case_definitions.name: configure-deploy
case_definitions.task: Detect our deploy platform and write the production URL, health checks, and deploy commands into project docs.

case_definitions.name: post-deploy-monitor
case_definitions.task: Watch the live app after deploy for console errors, page failures, and regressions.

case_definitions.name: performance-regression
case_definitions.task: Establish a page-load and Core Web Vitals baseline and compare this PR against it.

case_definitions.name: docs-after-release
case_definitions.task: Update README, architecture docs, and changelog to match what just shipped.

case_definitions.name: weekly-retro
case_definitions.task: Summarize what shipped this week across commits, tests, and team work patterns.

case_definitions.name: real-chrome
case_definitions.task: Launch my real Chrome with the side panel so I can watch the agent operate in the browser.

case_definitions.name: auth-cookies
case_definitions.task: Import cookies from my real browser so the headless browser can test authenticated pages.

case_definitions.name: careful-mode
case_definitions.task: Be careful while touching production and warn me before destructive shell commands.

case_definitions.name: freeze-edits
case_definitions.task: Only edit files under packages/api while debugging this issue.

case_definitions.name: full-guard-mode
case_definitions.task: Lock edits to this directory and warn before destructive commands.

case_definitions.name: unfreeze-edits
case_definitions.task: Remove the edit restriction so changes can touch the whole repo again.

case_definitions.name: upgrade-gstack
case_definitions.task: Upgrade gstack to the latest version and show me what changed.

case_definitions.name: no-tool-general-answer
case_definitions.task: Explain what a monorepo is in two paragraphs. Do not run a workflow.

arguments.rationale: The user asked a general question and explicitly said not to run a workflow.

choice.rationale: native functionCall

arguments.request: Open http://localhost:3000, click through the signup flow, and capture screenshots of any bug evidence.

choice.rationale: The user wants to perform a QA task in a browser, which involves navigating to a URL, interacting with elements (clicking through a signup flow), and capturing evidence of bugs via screenshots. The `gstack_gstack` tool is designed for exactly this purpose, as its description states it is a "Fast headless browser for QA testing and site dogfooding" that can "Navigate pages, interact with elements, ... and capture bug evidence."

arguments.request: QA this staging site, fix the bugs you find, add regression tests, and commit each fix atomically.

choice.rationale: The user's request is a direct and explicit match for the `gstack_qa` workflow. The user asks to 'QA' a site, 'fix the bugs', and 'commit each fix atomically'. The `gstack_qa` tool's purpose is described as: 'Systematically QA test a web application and fix bugs found. Runs QA testing, then iteratively fixes bugs in source code, committing each fix atomically and re-verifying.' This aligns perfectly with all components of the user's request.

arguments.request: Test this app and give me a bug report with repro steps, but do not edit any files.

choice.rationale: The user wants to test an application and receive a bug report with reproduction steps, but explicitly requested that no files be edited. The `gstack_qa_only` tool is designed for this exact purpose, as it runs QA tests and generates a report without making any code changes. The `gstack_qa` tool, in contrast, would attempt to fix the bugs it finds, which violates the user's constraint.

arguments.request: Audit the implemented dashboard UI for spacing, hierarchy, and visual slop, then fix the issues.

choice.rationale: The user wants to perform a visual audit on an existing UI, specifically checking for issues with spacing, hierarchy, and general visual quality ('slop'), and then apply fixes for these issues. The `gstack_design_review` tool is explicitly designed for this workflow: 'Designer's eye QA: finds visual inconsistency, spacing issues, hierarchy problems... then fixes them.' The tool's purpose directly matches the user's request to both audit and fix design issues on a live site.

arguments.request: Review this UI plan before implementation and score the design dimensions with recommendations.

choice.rationale: The user explicitly asks to 'Review this UI plan before implementation' and 'score the design dimensions'. The `gstack_plan_design_review` tool is designed for this exact scenario. Its purpose is described as a 'Designer's eye plan review' that 'Rates each design dimension 0-10, explains what would make it a 10, then fixes the plan to get there. Works in plan mode.' This directly matches the user's request for a pre-implementation review with scoring and recommendations.

arguments.request: Create a design system and DESIGN.md for a new B2B workflow product.

choice.rationale: The user's request to 'Create a design system and DESIGN.md' directly matches the purpose of the `gstack_design_consultation` tool, which is designed to 'propose a complete design system' and 'create DESIGN.md'. The tool's 'use_when' clause explicitly lists 'design system' and 'create DESIGN.md' as triggers.

arguments.request: Show me several visual directions for this feature and let me compare options before choosing.

choice.rationale: The user wants to see multiple visual design options for a feature and compare them. The `gstack_design_shotgun` tool is specifically designed for this purpose, as its description states it will "generate multiple AI design variants, open a comparison board, collect structured feedback, and iterate." The `use_when` triggers like "explore designs" and "show me options" directly match the user's intent.

arguments.request: I have an idea for a restaurant waitlist tool. Help me pressure-test whether it is worth building.

choice.rationale: The user has a new product idea and wants to evaluate if it's "worth building". This directly matches the purpose of the `gstack_office_hours` tool, which is designed to help users think through new ideas, brainstorm, and pressure-test concepts before any code is written. The tool's trigger phrases include "I have an idea" and "is this worth building", which align perfectly with the user's request.

arguments.request: Turn this rough feature request into a spec, pressure-test it, then validate the critical path in the browser.

choice.rationale: The user wants to convert a feature request into a specification, test that specification, and then validate the implementation in a browser. The `gstack_spec_qa` tool is designed for this exact workflow, as described in its purpose: "Turns a rough feature request into a concrete spec, pressure-tests the spec, and validates the critical path in a real browser".

arguments.request: Rethink this plan from a founder perspective and tell me whether the scope is ambitious enough.

choice.rationale: The user wants to review a plan from a "founder perspective" and determine if the "scope is ambitious enough." The `gstack_plan_ceo_review` tool is explicitly designed for this, described as a "CEO/founder-mode plan review" that can "expand scope" and is triggered by requests like "rethink this" or "is this ambitious enough".

arguments.request: Review the architecture, data flow, edge cases, and test plan before implementation.

choice.rationale: The user's request to 'Review the architecture, data flow, edge cases, and test plan before implementation' directly matches the purpose of the `gstack_plan_eng_review` tool. This tool is specifically designed for an 'Eng manager-mode plan review' that locks in the execution plan, including architecture, data flow, and test coverage, before coding starts.

arguments.request: Run CEO, design, and engineering plan reviews automatically and surface only the key decisions.

choice.rationale: The user's request to 'Run CEO, design, and engineering plan reviews automatically and surface only the key decisions' directly maps to the `gstack_autoplan` tool's purpose. The tool is described as an 'Auto-review pipeline' that 'reads the full CEO, design, and eng review skills from disk and runs them sequentially with auto-decisions' and 'Surfaces taste decisions... at a final approval gate.' This is a precise match for the user's workflow.

arguments.request: Review this PR before merge for SQL safety, trust boundaries, and production bugs.

choice.rationale: The user explicitly asked to 'Review this PR before merge' and mentioned specific checks like 'SQL safety' and 'trust boundaries'. The `gstack_review` tool is designed for 'Pre-landing PR review' and its purpose includes analyzing the diff for 'SQL safety, LLM trust boundary violations, conditional side effects, and other structural issues', which is a direct match for the user's request.

arguments.request: Debug why this job started failing and do not patch anything until you find the root cause.

choice.rationale: The user wants to debug a failing job and explicitly find the root cause before implementing any fixes. The `gstack_investigate` tool is designed for "Systematic debugging with root cause investigation" and its "Iron Law" is "no fixes without root cause," which directly aligns with the user's request. The `use_when` triggers include "debug this", "why is this broken", and "root cause analysis".

arguments.request: Run an infrastructure-first security audit with secrets, dependency supply chain, OWASP, and STRIDE coverage.

choice.rationale: The user is asking for a comprehensive security audit, specifically mentioning 'infrastructure-first', 'secrets', 'dependency supply chain', 'OWASP', and 'STRIDE'. The `gstack_cso` tool is designed for exactly this purpose, as its description states it performs an 'Infrastructure-first security audit: secrets archaeology, dependency supply chain... plus OWASP Top 10, STRIDE threat modeling'.

arguments.request: Sync main, run tests, update changelog, push this branch, and create a PR.

choice.rationale: The user wants to perform a series of actions to prepare and ship their code: syncing with the main branch, running tests, updating the changelog, pushing the branch, and creating a pull request. The `gstack_ship` tool is designed for this exact workflow, as its purpose is to "detect + merge base branch, run tests, review diff, bump VERSION, update CHANGELOG, commit, push, create PR." The keywords in the request ("tests", "changelog", "push", "create a PR") directly map to the capabilities of the `gstack_ship` tool.

arguments.request: Merge the approved PR, wait for CI and deploy, then verify production health.

choice.rationale: The user wants to merge a PR, deploy it, and verify it in production. The `gstack_land_and_deploy` tool is explicitly designed for this workflow, as it 'Merges the PR, waits for CI and deploy, verifies production health via canary checks'.

arguments.request: Detect our deploy platform and write the production URL, health checks, and deploy commands into project docs.

choice.rationale: The user wants to detect their deployment platform and configure settings like the production URL, health checks, and deploy commands, and then save this information. The `gstack_setup_deploy` tool is explicitly designed for this purpose: "Configure deployment settings for /land-and-deploy. Detects your deploy platform... production URL, health check endpoints, and deploy status commands. Writes the configuration to CLAUDE.md". This is a direct match for the user's request.

arguments.request: Watch the live app after deploy for console errors, page failures, and regressions.

choice.rationale: The user's request is to monitor a live application after a deployment for errors, failures, and regressions. The `gstack_canary` tool is designed for 'Post-deploy canary monitoring' and its purpose is to 'Watches the live app for console errors, performance regressions, and page failures', which is a direct match for the user's task.

arguments.request: Establish a page-load and Core Web Vitals baseline and compare this PR against it.

choice.rationale: The user's request to 'Establish a page-load and Core Web Vitals baseline and compare this PR against it' directly maps to the functionality of the `gstack_benchmark` tool. The tool's purpose is described as 'Performance regression detection... Establishes baselines for page load times, Core Web Vitals... Compares before/after on every PR.' The keywords in the request ('page-load', 'Core Web Vitals', 'baseline', 'compare...PR') are all present in the tool's description.

arguments.request: Update README, architecture docs, and changelog to match what just shipped.

choice.rationale: The user wants to update several documentation files (README, architecture docs, changelog) to reflect recent changes that have been 'shipped'. The `gstack_document_release` tool is explicitly designed for this purpose, described as a 'Post-ship documentation update' that 'updates README/ARCHITECTURE... to match what shipped, polishes CHANGELOG voice'. This is a direct match for the user's request.

arguments.request: Summarize what shipped this week across commits, tests, and team work patterns.

choice.rationale: The user is asking for a summary of what shipped during the week, including an analysis of commits and team work patterns. The `gstack_retro` tool is designed for exactly this purpose, described as a "Weekly engineering retrospective" that "Analyzes commit history, work patterns, and code quality metrics" and is triggered by requests like "what did we ship".

arguments.request: Launch my real Chrome with the side panel so I can watch the agent operate in the browser.

choice.rationale: The user wants to launch a 'real Chrome with the side panel' to 'watch the agent operate'. The `gstack_connect_chrome` tool is specifically designed to 'Launch real Chrome controlled by gstack with the Side Panel extension auto-loaded' so the user can 'watch every action in real time'. This is a direct match for the user's request.

arguments.request: Import cookies from my real browser so the headless browser can test authenticated pages.
